title: Merck — Transplant — Synthesis
html:
Merck Transplant Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-26 Studies analyzed: 3 Files parsed: Message Recall Study KT + HSCT Full Report v4, Prevymis KT PET Full Report v1, Prevymis ATU Proposal v2.0 Findings extracted: Manual extraction from ~185K chars of parsed report content across 3 study deliverables Method: Cross-study pattern matching against Vinoth's Merck transplant thesis tracker

1. Overview

This synthesis integrates findings from three ZoomRx studies conducted for Merck's Prevymis transplant franchise in 2024-2025. The studies span two transplant types (kidney transplant and HSCT), two methodologies (PET/message recall and ATU proposal), and cover the critical post-KT-launch period when Merck is simultaneously expanding the KT franchise and maintaining HSCT leadership.

The central question across all studies: Can Prevymis overcome the generic valganciclovir price barrier to capture the much larger kidney transplant market, and what is the promotional strategy that will accelerate this?

2. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: The Tolerability Story Sells — but the Efficacy Story Converts

This is the most strategically important finding across the portfolio.

Message Recall Study (v4): KT message recall shows safety/tolerability messages (KT9: leukopenia 26% vs. 64%) are the most recalled AND highly effective. This makes them the natural opening for reps. However, the most effective KT message overall is KT7 ("No CMV cases with Prevymis vs. 5 cases with valganciclovir") — and it is also significantly associated with high-impact interactions that drive prescribing intent. Yet KT7 has the lowest recall of all KT messages.

PET Report (v1): Confirms the same pattern. Efficacy topics are the #1 unaided recall (>40% of HCPs mention discussing efficacy), but the specific "no CMV cases" claim is under-delivered relative to its impact. HCPs who received new efficacy information were most likely to change their prescribing behavior.

ATU Proposal (v2.0): The study design explicitly separates stated vs. derived importance of attributes. This framework reveals that safety/tolerability is the stated differentiator (what HCPs say matters), but efficacy is the derived driver (what statistically predicts prescribing). This stated-derived gap is characteristic of markets where the category is mature and the new entrant needs to shift the value equation.

What this means together: Merck's reps are leading with what's comfortable (safety) rather than what's most impactful (efficacy). This is understandable — the safety story is simple and universally resonant. But the conversion event (moving an HCP from "aware" to "prescribing") requires the efficacy story. The promotional strategy should be: safety opens the door, efficacy closes the deal.

Implication for Merck: Rep training should emphasize transitioning from safety opener to efficacy close within a single detail. The KT7 message should be positioned as the "closer" after KT9 establishes the safety frame. ZoomRx should track KT7 delivery rates as the key leading indicator of promotional effectiveness.

Pattern 2: KT Is Prioritized Over HSCT in Multi-Indication Details — Confirming the Strategic Shift

Message Recall Study (v4): Among ID physicians and pharmacists at dual KT/HSCT institutions, 75% receive both-indication discussions. Within these multi-indication interactions, KT is consistently prioritized: KT discussions are initiated first and receive more time. This is not accidental — it reflects Merck's strategic priority to grow the newer, larger KT indication.

PET Report (v1): Consistent finding — KT discussions initiated first and given more time in dual-indication calls.

ATU Proposal (v2.0): The proposal focuses exclusively on KT, with Wave 2 tracking KT-specific metrics. This signals that Merck's commercial team views KT as the growth engine requiring the most research investment.

What this means together: The HSCT franchise is mature and largely managed. KT is the growth story. But HSCT cannot be neglected — HSCT efficacy messages (survival benefit, 200-day efficacy) are highly effective when delivered but have low recall rates. There is a risk of HSCT promotional underperformance if all attention shifts to KT.

Implication for Merck: Maintain HSCT as a "sustain" priority while investing growth resources in KT. Monitor HSCT message recall and prescribing intent for signs of erosion. The 200-day extended dosing message needs more delivery focus in HSCT.

Pattern 3: Access and Cost Are the Unspoken Elephant — and HCPs Are Asking for Help

Message Recall Study (v4): Access and savings messages (MAP, coupon, $15 co-pay) consistently appear in the "low recall, low effectiveness" quadrant. Yet HCPs actively say they want to hear more about insurance/reimbursement. The disconnect: HCPs want practical access help (how do I get this drug for my patient?), not promotional access messages (eligible patients may pay as little as $15).

PET Report (v1): Identical pattern. Access topics have low recall despite HCPs wanting more information on insurance. Pharmacists in particular want more safety and access information.

ATU Proposal (v2.0): The proposal includes access-related prescribing factors and identifies "low out-of-pocket cost for patients" and "low MD burden for reimbursement" as key derived drivers. This confirms that access is not just a promotional afterthought — it is a structural driver of prescribing behavior.

What this means together: The current access messaging is not landing because it is framed as promotional ("$15 co-pay!") rather than solution-oriented ("here's how to navigate prior auth for your patient"). HCPs are experiencing real friction getting Prevymis prescribed and want practical support, not savings card advertisements.

Implication for Merck: Reframe access messaging from promotional to consultative. Train reps to offer hands-on prior authorization support. Position the MAP as a clinical support service, not a financial program. The pharmacist segment is particularly underserved on access and could be a multiplier if engaged effectively.

Pattern 4: The Community-Academic Split Suggests Different Growth Strategies

Message Recall Study (v4):

  • Community HCPs: Higher perceived effectiveness of messages, higher call quality ratings, more responsive to savings/access messages
  • Academic HCPs: Higher prescribing intent, higher unaided recall of clinical topics, more responsive to trial design and clinical data

PET Report (v1): Academic HCPs recalled efficacy (67%) and safety (64%) at higher rates than community HCPs (54% and 54%). Community HCPs were more responsive to the pediatric indication (49% vs. 30% recall).

What this means together: Community and academic transplant centers operate differently. Community centers make faster individual decisions but may have more access barriers. Academic centers are slower (formulary committees, protocol committees) but once adopted, Prevymis becomes institutional standard. The promotional approach should differ: community needs simple, compelling messages + access support; academic needs clinical evidence + institutional champion development.

3. Gaps and Research Opportunities

Gap 1: No Institutional-Level Research

All current studies measure HCP-level attitudes and behaviors. But the real adoption bottleneck for a $225/day drug vs. a $15/day generic may be at the formulary/institutional level. No study currently captures P&T committee dynamics, formulary restriction status, or institutional protocol adoption.

Recommendation: Propose institutional formulary tracking study — 20-30 top transplant centers, quarterly monitoring of Prevymis formulary status, restriction level, and protocol integration.

Gap 2: No Longitudinal Prescribing Tracking

The ATU provides cross-sectional snapshots. But Prevymis KT is in a launch adoption curve where trajectory matters more than point-in-time share. Without longitudinal tracking, we cannot distinguish "growing adoption" from "stalled at early adopters."

Recommendation: Ensure Wave 3+ ATU is designed to track the same HCPs over time, enabling cohort-based adoption analysis.

Gap 3: No Competitive Intelligence on Livtencity Strategy

Livtencity (maribavir, Takeda) is not a direct prophylaxis competitor today, but it competes for HCP attention and SOV. If Takeda pursues prophylaxis trials or expands into first-line treatment, Prevymis's competitive moat narrows.

Recommendation: Include Livtencity pipeline monitoring in quarterly briefing reviews. Flag any Phase 3 expansion trials immediately.

4. Summary: What Vinoth Should Tell Merck

"Your KT promotional engine is strong — 75% high-quality interactions, >50% prescribing intent, clear SOV leadership. But you're leaving money on the table in three places: (1) Your most effective message — 'no CMV cases vs. valganciclovir' — is your least delivered. Fix this and you move the needle on conversions. (2) Your access messaging is promotional when it should be consultative — HCPs are struggling with prior auths, not unaware of the co-pay card. (3) You have no visibility into the formulary decisions that actually determine whether HCPs CAN prescribe, regardless of whether they WANT to. We can help with all three."
